judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports hit all four expectations with comparable rigor; Report 2 adds slightly more precision (malformed-value handling, test specifics, grep verification of all consumers) but both are equally responsive to the task.

report_1.evidence: The decision is made in `auto_send_reminders_enabled` in `backend/app/feature_flags.py` — specifically lines 11–12: `if tenant.settings.get("auto_send_reminders") is None: return True`.

report_2.evidence: The decision point is `auto_send_reminders_enabled` in `slotwise/backend/app/feature_flags.py:10-13`... `feature_flags.py:12` — `return True` — is the exact line that decides it.

report_1.evidence: The fix is to change `backend/app/feature_flags.py:12` from `return True` to `return False` (or more simply, make the function `return bool(tenant.settings.get("auto_send_reminders"))`)

report_2.evidence: The fix is at `feature_flags.py:11-13`: replace the `is None → True` branch so that missing (and malformed) values resolve to `False` — e.g. `return tenant.settings.get("auto_send_reminders") is True`

report_1.evidence: Default-on is defensible for `compact_calendar_view`: it's a cosmetic UI preference where "on unless the tenant opted out" is harmless and probably the desired rollout. Reusing that default for a flag that triggers outbound SMS/email to patients transplants a low-stakes convention into a high-stakes context without re-examining it.

report_2.evidence: Commit `25509a7` (SLOT-190, 2026-06-10) introduced `compact_calendar_view_enabled` with the `is None → return True` pattern — a reasonable grandfather-on choice for a calendar display preference... inheriting its null-semantics across a category boundary — from "UI preference" to "automated outbound action" — is a product decision that was skipped, not made.

report_1.evidence: Add a global kill switch above the per-tenant flag. `run()` in `reminders.py` gates only per-tenant; there is no environment-level or deployment-level gate to stop all sends at once if something goes wrong (e.g. a bad template or a messaging-provider incident). `infra/main.tf` defines only an `environment` variable — no feature-flag infrastructure.

report_2.evidence: Add an env-level master kill switch ANDed with the per-tenant value in `reminders.py`'s gate, with a no-op default, so there is a single rollback affordance that stops all sending without touching every tenant row. Today the only gate is the per-tenant value at `reminders.py:25`; `infra/main.tf` defines nothing for this feature, so there is no documented off switch.
